document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide
root.summary:
Front matter and main content for the Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide. It targets business analysts in social enterprise organizations and explains the client-centric outcome management approach for clients referred to employment/outcomes agencies. The guide covers purpose, audience, prerequisites, and related guides, then walks through outcome services referrals, self-sufficiency assessments, advisements, outcome plan creation/approval/closure, client agreements, attendance and participation tracking, discrepancy handling, case reviews, and productivity tools.

It also describes worker roles (eligibility worker, outcome worker, supervisor), integration with the Income Support case, support for single or multi-person plans, and configurable summaries for overdue assessments and discrepancies. The section includes the contents page and end matter: notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and legal/copyright terms.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note tells readers to review the Notices section on page 35 before using the product or this documentation. It also shows the copyright information for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Edition and front matter for the Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide. States the edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with Merative copyright/trademark notices and privacy policy. The early pages also outline the guide’s purpose, audience, prerequisites, and chapter list, and introduce the product’s scope: outcome management, referrals, assessments, advisements, outcome plans, participation tracking, and productivity tools.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Covers the A&T “Edition” content for Return to Work Outcome Management: referral handling, assessment management, advisements, and outcome plan building. Describes how pending referrals are created, assigned, completed, or canceled; introduces the Self-Sufficiency assessment with nine factors, scoring, graphs, deadlines, prioritization, reassessment, and history/reporting; explains advisements sent between outcome and eligibility workers; and outlines outcome plans and pre-defined activities/services for client goal tracking.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Covers Return to Work Outcome Management from selecting and scheduling plan activities through federal component mapping, plan approval/closure, client agreements, and monitoring client eligibility changes. Describes participation tracking, attendance rosters, excused absences, monthly/weekly participation summaries, discrepancy batch checks and resolution, and case review cycles. Also outlines productivity tools for outcome workers and supervisors, including assigned referrals, assessments due, pending discrepancies, overdue assessments, and overdue discrepancy summaries. Useful for understanding operational workflow and reporting rules.

Front matter and main content for the Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide, applicable to Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2. Introduces the guide’s purpose, audience, prerequisites, and chapter structure, then covers outcome management overview, referrals, self-sufficiency assessments, advisements, outcome plans, participation tracking, discrepancy handling, case reviews, and productivity tools for workers and supervisors. Includes operational details such as assignment, approval/closure, attendance summaries, batch discrepancy checks, and overdue work summaries.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Contains legal notices: fictitious names disclaimer, Merative client agreement terms, sample code copyright license and “AS IS” warranty disclaimer, liability limitation, privacy policy link, trademark notices for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and a copyright notice (2012, 2024).

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that all example names in the document are fictitious, and that sample programs are provided by Merative under the Client Agreement with an “as is” warranty disclaimer. Includes a privacy policy URL and a trademarks notice listing Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX marks, plus a note that other names may be trademarks. Also shows copyright notice: © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

Legal notices and publication terms: personal and enterprise use are allowed only with proprietary notices preserved, but redistribution, derivative works, export, and external use are restricted without Merative consent. Includes broad “AS IS” warranty disclaimer, no guarantee of content accuracy, patent notice, third-party product caveats, non-endorsement of external websites, and a copyright notice (© Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024).

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide (v8.1.2), listing the guide’s major chapters and page numbers. Covers introduction, outcome management concepts and roles, referrals, self-sufficiency assessments, advisements, outcome plans, client participation monitoring, and productivity tools, plus notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful for quickly locating relevant sections in the guide.

children.title: 1 Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management Guide
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: This section states that the guide provides a business-level overview of Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management. It explains that the guide is for business analysts in social enterprise organizations, assumes familiarity with SEM, Cúram, and Income Support, and is not a training or user guide. It also says readers should use the full guide to understand how outcome management helps identify client needs and achieve self-sufficiency, and lists related prerequisite guides.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for business analysts in a social enterprise organization, this section says the guide assumes familiarity with Social Enterprise Management (SEM), Cúram basics, and Income Support concepts. It also lists useful prerequisite reading: Provider Management, Income Support Cash Assistance, Income Support Food Assistance, and Cúram Outcome Management Support and Configuration guides. This section helps readers judge whether they have the background needed to use the guide effectively.

children.title: Prerequisites
children.summary: This section states the guide’s prerequisites: readers should already know the basic Cúram product and Income Support concepts. It also recommends reviewing related guides on Provider Management, Income Support Cash Assistance, Income Support Food Assistance, Cúram Outcome Management Support, and Cúram Outcome Management Configuration. The section frames the document as a business-level overview for business analysts in social enterprise organizations with SEM knowledge, not a training or user guide.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This section introduces the Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management guide, which explains a client-centric approach to service delivery and case management focused on helping clients and households achieve positive outcomes and self-sufficiency. It states the guide’s purpose, intended audience (business analysts in social enterprise organizations), and prerequisites, including familiarity with Cúram, Income Support, and related guides. It also begins the chapter list, pointing to “What is Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management?”

This introduction says the guide is a business-level overview of Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management. It targets business analysts in social enterprise organizations and assumes familiarity with SEM, Cúram basics, and Income Support concepts. It is not a training or user guide. The section also recommends related prerequisite reading: Provider Management, Income Support Cash Assistance, Income Support Food Assistance, and Cúram Outcome Management Support and Configuration guides. It points readers to the guide’s chapter on what the product is.

children.title: 1.2 What is Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management?
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces Cúram Return to Work Outcome Management and its role in client-centric case management for employment/outcomes agencies. Summarizes the chapter set: outcome services referrals, self-sufficiency assessments, advisements, building outcome plans, monitoring client participation, and productivity tools. Explains that the module uses Income Support, Provider Management, and assessment/tracking content to support integrated case management, including assessment-based recommendations, client agreements, and progress monitoring toward self-sufficiency goals.

children.title: The Roles of Eligibility Worker and Outcome Worker
children.summary: Explains how eligibility workers and outcome workers are split in Cúram Income Support Return to Work Outcome Management. Eligibility workers determine eligibility and refer clients; outcome workers receive referrals and manage assessments, plans, agreements, advisements, participation, attendance discrepancies, and progress toward self-sufficiency. Notes that the product supports either role, with separate worker configurations, and that supervisors can perform outcome worker tasks plus supervisory duties. Also introduces pre-defined assessment and tracking content for Income Support.

children.title: Supervisor Role
children.summary: The supervisor role is for outcome worker supervisors who manage an organizational unit’s outcomes workload. Supervisors can do everything an outcome worker can, plus use supervisory functions and receive task escalations when needed. This section sits within the Return to Work Outcome Management guide, which also describes pre-defined assessment and tracking content for Income Support.

children.title: Pre-Defined Outcome Management Content
children.summary: This section introduces pre-defined Return to Work Outcome Management content for Income Support. It explains that the product includes built-in business processes and sample content for assessments, outcome plans, activities, referrals, advisements, monitoring, and worker productivity tools, with sample Cash Assistance and limited Food Assistance content. It also outlines the configurable A&T model, the outcome worker and eligibility worker roles, supervisor responsibilities, and how referrals, assessments, service plans, participation tracking, and attendance discrepancies are managed.

children.title: Integration with the Income Support Case
children.summary: This section explains how Return to Work Outcome Management and Assessment and Tracking integrate with the Income Support integrated case. It covers sanction advisements, automatic notifications, and eligibility worker work referrals. It also describes the work referral process, including eligibility checks, locating nearby outcome agencies using geocoding, and notifications for non-registration. Additional topics include attendance tracking by providers or outcome workers, real-time participation monitoring, federal component hour tracking, and support for single or multi-person plans.

children.title: Support for the Work Referral Process
children.summary: Describes the work referral workflow in Assessment and Tracking. Eligibility workers run check eligibility to identify work-eligible clients, search for nearby outcome agencies using geocoding, and create referrals. Outcome workers can send advisements back to eligibility workers, and eligibility workers are notified if a client fails to register by deadline. The section also notes geocode must be enabled for referrals and that A&T supports creating single or combined plans for multiple referred household members.

children.title: Support for Attendance Tracking Process
children.summary: This section describes attendance tracking in Assessment and Tracking. It lets outcome workers view real-time client attendance, while providers can submit attendance via self-service or paper roster, and workers can enter it directly when no provider exists. The system notifies workers when actual participation hours fall short of scheduled hours and tracks hours toward core and non-core federal components for participation-rate monitoring.

children.title: Ability to Create Single or Multi-person Plans
children.summary: Assessment and Tracking includes flexible plan creation for referred household members. When multiple people from the same household are referred to an outcomes agency, the system can either create a separate plan for each person or combine all referred members into one shared plan.

Overview of Cúram Income Support Return to Work Outcome Management. It describes a client-centric, integrated case-management approach for employment/outcomes agencies, using Assessment and Tracking plus Income Support and Provider Management. The section explains the roles of eligibility worker, outcome worker, and supervisor; built-in assessment, plan, advisement, monitoring, and productivity content; integration with sanctions and automatic notifications; work referral and geocoding; attendance tracking and participation-hour monitoring; and support for single or multi-person plans.

children.title: 1.3 Managing Outcome Services Referrals
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces managing outcome services referrals in Cúram Income Support. Explains that many Cash Assistance/Food Assistance clients must register for work, though some are exempt (for example, children, elderly, certain parents, and adults with disabilities). Describes how eligibility workers generate internal referrals to outcome workers so clients can plan work or education activities. Defines referral-related evidence: Non Participation Reason and Work Registration. Summarizes CER-based Work Eligibility status, which determines who should be referred and can coexist with a Non Participation Reason.

children.title: Outcome Services Referral
children.summary: Explains how eligibility workers create Outcome Services Referrals in Cúram Income Support to send clients to an internal outcomes agency for work registration and planning. Covers when referrals are used (new applications and reassessments), notes Food Assistance referral process is not configured, and distinguishes this internal referral from external agency referrals. Also defines related evidence types, Non Participation Reason and Work Registration, and describes how CER Work Eligibility determines who must be referred, including the Work Eligible status and its interaction with non-participation reasons.

children.title: Determining Who is Work Eligible
children.summary: Explains how eligibility workers determine who must be referred for work registration/outcome services. It introduces CER Work Eligibility results, which appear on eligibility decisions and a Work Eligibility tab, and notes that “Work Eligible” means the client has no exclusion from work registration or work plan participation for Cash Assistance or Food Assistance. Also clarifies that a client may be Work Eligible and still have a Non Participation Reason.

children.title: Referring Clients
children.summary: Explains when and how Work Eligible clients are referred to outcomes workers. Referrals can be created immediately after eligibility determines Cash Assistance eligibility, without waiting for authorization, and are unnecessary if the household is ineligible or the client has a Non Participation Reason. The eligibility worker refers clients individually using display data such as work eligibility status, current and recommended non participation reasons, and work registration status. Also notes search support for finding a nearby outcomes agency office and informing clients of registration deadlines.

children.title: After Referrals are Made
children.summary: Explains what happens after a client referral is made: the outcome worker finds the pending referral, sees key details (client, case, active plan, date referred, referrer, status, deadline), and can assign it to self so the referral is in progress. Referrals are handled per individual, not by household. The eligibility worker can see when the client visits, the outcome worker may create an advisement, and work registration is recorded in Evidence. Also covers canceling a referral if the client changes their mind.

children.title: Cancelling Referrals based on Client Wishes
children.summary: Explains that if a client changes their mind after accepting an outcome-worker referral, the eligibility worker cancels the referral. If the client has not yet visited the outcomes agency, the cancelled referral can no longer be selected by any outcome worker.

Explains how eligibility workers manage internal Outcome Services Referrals in Cúram Income Support for Cash Assistance/Food Assistance clients who must register for work. Covers exempt vs. work-eligible clients, CER Work Eligibility results, and related evidence types: Non Participation Reason and Work Registration. Describes when referrals are created (new applications or reassessments, before authorization), that Food Assistance referral processing is not configured, and how workers select individuals, use office search, and inform clients of registration deadlines.

children.title: 1.4 Managing Self-Sufficiency Assessments
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces Managing Self-Sufficiency Assessments in Cúram A&T for social enterprise agencies. It explains that assessments help identify client needs and support planning toward self-sufficiency. The system offers one configured assessment type, Self-Sufficiency, using Intelligent Evidence Gathering (IEG) to collect answers and Cúram Eligibility Rules (CER) to calculate results. These results help the client and outcome worker determine desired outcomes during the planning process.

children.title: A&T Assessment Initial Data
Assessment Types
Assessment Factors
children.summary: This section explains that the Self-Sufficiency assessment in A&T uses nine initial client need factors: Employment, Education, Child Care, Transportation, Housing, Substance Abuse, Physical Health, Mental Health, and Domestic Violence. It briefly describes what each factor measures, mainly focusing on barriers to work, self-sufficiency, safety, and the need for further assessment or treatment.

children.title: Assessment Questions by Factor
children.summary: This section lists the Self-Sufficiency Assessment script questions and scoring rules for all factors used in Cúram Income Support return-to-work outcome management. It covers Employment, Education, Child Care, Transportation, Housing, Substance Abuse, Physical Health, Mental Health, and Domestic Violence. For each factor, it shows conditional follow-up questions and answer scores used to compute outcomes. Useful for locating the exact assessment prompts and scoring logic.

children.title: Assessment Scoring
children.summary: This section explains assessment scoring for Self-Sufficiency and A&T programs. It defines the 10-point rating bands: Vulnerable (0–2), Engaged (3–5), Progressing (6–8), and Self-Sufficient/Safe (9–10), also called barriers categories. It describes running assessments from within a plan, auto-derived factor questions, manual score entry when external parties assess factors, and how results are displayed after rules execute. It also notes available visualization options: Bar Chart and Radar Chart, with a configurable default.

Defines the A&T Self-Sufficiency assessment used in Return to Work Outcome Management. It explains that agencies can run multiple assessment types, but this product is configured for individual client assessments tied to an integrated case and outcome plan. The section lists the nine assessment factors and what each measures, then provides the script questions and scoring logic for each factor, including follow-up questions. Useful for finding assessment prompts, answer scores, and factor-based self-sufficiency ratings.

Defines the preconfigured A&T Self-Sufficiency assessment used in Return to Work Outcome Management. It explains that agencies may run multiple assessment types, but this product is set up for individual client assessments tied to an integrated case and outcome plan. It lists the nine assessment factors—employment, education, child care, transportation, housing, substance abuse, physical health, mental health, and domestic violence—and provides the factor questions, answer scoring, and follow-up logic. Useful for locating assessment content, prompts, and scoring rules.

children.title: Running an Assessment to Identify Client Needs
children.summary: Explains how an outcome worker initiates a client assessment from within a plan, with questions displayed and results generated after all factor questions are answered. Notes that agencies may assess all or only some of the nine Self-Sufficiency factors, sometimes relying on external assessors and manual score entry. Also covers assessment scoring rules: a shared 10-point scale with ratings Vulnerable (0–2), Engaged (3–5), Progressing (6–8), and Self-Sufficient/Safe (9–10). Mentions results display with configurable Bar Chart or Radar Chart views.

children.title: Answering Factor Questions
children.summary: Explains how factor questions are answered and scored in Cúram assessments. It notes that automatically derived factors have associated questions, but agencies may assess only some of the nine Self-Sufficiency factors or use external parties, with manual score entry possible. It also covers assessment execution and results display, including four rating categories on a ten-point scale (Vulnerable 0–2, Engaged 3–5, Progressing 6–8, Self-Sufficient/Safe 9–10) and available Bar Chart/Radar Chart visualizations.

children.title: Scoring Assessments
children.summary: This section explains assessment scoring and workflow in Cúram 8.1.2. It describes the 10-point scoring scale for Self-Sufficiency factors, with ratings of Vulnerable (0–2), Engaged (3–5), Progressing (6–8), and Self-Sufficient/Safe (9–10), also called barriers categories. It covers how outcome workers initiate and complete assessments, that some agencies may assess only selected factors or use external parties, and that scores may be manually entered. It also notes that assessment results are shown with Bar Chart and Radar Chart views.

children.title: Viewing Assessment Status
children.summary: This section explains how users view assessment status in the system. It notes that assessment results may be automatically scored from factor questions or manually entered from an external score, with classifications such as Vulnerable, Engaged, Progressing, and Self-Sufficient/Safe. It also states that the system shows when an assessment is due and its deadline, with a configured due period of 60 days. Related topics include factor prioritization after assessments and viewing assessment reports with summary, results, and reassessment details.

children.title: Prioritizing Factors After an Assessment
children.summary: This section explains how factor scores are set automatically from assessment question answers or entered manually from external scores, and how classifications are derived. It also notes that the system shows assessment due status with a 60-day deadline. Most importantly, it describes factor prioritization after an assessment: outcome workers can mark urgent factors for a specific person and assessment to focus on the most pressing needs, such as housing, before others. It briefly covers the assessment report, including summaries, factor results, scores, and priority status.

children.title: View Assessment Report
children.summary: This section explains how assessment results are scored and displayed in the report. It covers automatic scoring from factor-question answers versus manually entered external scores, with classifications such as Vulnerable, Engaged, Progressing, and Self-Sufficient/Safe. It also notes assessment due-date visibility and a 60-day deadline. The report view includes client summary details, current assessment results with scores and priority flags, and reassessment history showing factor-by-factor dates, classifications, scores, and priorities.

children.title: Rerunning an Assessment
children.summary: This section explains rerunning a completed assessment, where the reassessment date defaults from the assessment frequency, and viewing factor/history results to compare two assessments for the same client. It also introduces managing advisements: outcome workers can create pending advisements from the outcome plan to notify eligibility workers, including type, subject, plan reference, date, and text. Advisements cannot be modified after sending, and eligibility workers can accept or reject them with comments and status.

children.title: Viewing Factor History
children.summary: Explains “Viewing Factor History” for Self-Sufficiency assessments: outcome workers can compare two assessments for the same client to see how scores and answers changed over time. History is shown in a results list and is meant to give access to each assessment outcome. The page also transitions into “Managing Advisements,” covering how outcome workers create advisements to eligibility workers, what information they include, notification behavior, and how eligibility workers can accept or reject them with comments.

Explains how Return to Work Outcome Management handles Self-Sufficiency assessments in Cúram A&T. Covers the preconfigured individual assessment tied to a plan, the nine factors (employment through domestic violence), factor questions and scoring, and the four rating bands on a 10-point scale. Describes initiating an assessment from a plan, automatic or manual scoring, configurable Bar Chart/Radar Chart results, due status with a 60-day deadline, factor prioritization, and the assessment report showing summary, current results, and reassessment details.

children.title: 1.5 Managing Advisements
Introduction
children.summary: This section covers reassessing a completed assessment and viewing factor history to compare two Self-Sufficiency assessments, including results and answers over time. It then introduces Managing Advisements in Cúram A&T, explaining how outcome workers send tracked notifications/recommendations to eligibility workers from an outcome plan, with details like type, subject, plan reference, date, and text. Advisements are created in pending status, cannot be modified after sending, and the eligibility worker can accept or reject them with status and comments.

children.title: Creating Advisements
children.summary: Explains the A&T advisements feature for communication between outcome and eligibility workers. An outcome worker can create a pending advisement from an outcome plan, including type, subject, plan reference, date, and detailed text, which notifies the linked eligibility worker on the application or integrated case. Once sent, the outcome worker cannot edit it. The eligibility worker can view advisements in participation, then accept or reject them and add status/comments visible to the outcome worker.

children.title: Accepting or Rejecting Advisements
children.summary: Explains how eligibility workers handle advisements sent by outcome workers in Assessment and Tracking. After an outcome worker creates and sends a pending advisement from the outcome plan, it cannot be modified and notifies the associated eligibility worker. Within participation on the integrated case, the eligibility worker can view advisement status and either accept or reject the recommendation, adding a status and comments on rejection that are shown to the outcome worker.

children.title: Monitoring Advisements
children.summary: This section explains how outcome workers monitor all advisements on a case, including status and any rejection comments entered by the eligibility worker. It then introduces outcome plan building: identifying client needs, creating and updating plans from self-sufficiency assessment results, involving client sign-off, and managing plans tied to integrated cases. It also covers assigning and scheduling client activities, with flexibility for different agency procedures and grouping activities by federal component (core or non-core).

This section explains A&T advisements for communication between outcome and eligibility workers. Outcome workers can create a pending advisement from an outcome plan with type, subject, plan reference, date, and text; it notifies the linked eligibility worker and cannot be edited after sending. Eligibility workers can view advisements in participation on the integrated case, then accept or reject them with status and comments visible to the outcome worker. The page also briefly covers rerunning assessments and viewing factor history to compare results and answers over time.

children.title: 1.6 Building Outcome Plans to Achieve Positive Outcomes
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces building and managing outcome plans in Return to Work Outcome Management. Explains that outcome workers use Self-Sufficiency assessment information to create client plans, often selecting system-recommended activities, with client participation and sign-off. Covers how plans are opened from integrated cases, with flexibility for one or multiple referred members to have separate or shared plans. Also notes that most outcome work occurs within a plan and that activities (actions, services, referrals) are assigned to address barriers and may be grouped by federal component (core/non-core).

children.title: Managing Outcome Plans
Opening Outcome Plans
children.summary: Explains how outcome plans are created and managed for return-to-work clients. An outcome plan is tied to an integrated case, and referred members can be added to the plan either separately or together, depending on agency procedure. The section also covers the role of the outcome worker in using self-sufficiency assessment information to build a client-understandable plan, select recommended activities, involve client sign-off, and monitor/update plans. It introduces client activities and federal components for grouping activities by policy.

Explains how outcome workers manage outcome plans for return-to-work clients. Plans are created from pending referrals and are always tied to an integrated case, with referred members added separately or together depending on agency procedure. The section covers using self-sufficiency assessment information to build client-understandable plans, select recommended activities, and obtain client sign-off. It also notes that plans are monitored and updated regularly, and introduces client activities and federal components for grouping activities by policy.

children.title: Managing Client Activities
What are activities?
children.summary: Defines activities in A&T activity assignment. Activities can be actions, services, or referrals to external providers. Services have an associated provider maintained in Cúram Provider Management; actions do not. The section lists the predefined actions and services included in A&T content, covering child care, employment, education, job search, training, and related support activities.

children.title: Selecting Activities
children.summary: This section explains how the system selects and manages outcome plan activities. It covers recommended work activities based on Self-Sufficiency assessment results, adding suggested or non-suggested activities, and configuring activity mappings. It also describes scheduling action/service details such as dates, frequency, hours, days, cost, and federal component. In addition, it explains external referral handling, including provider details and referral letters, and how activities are associated with federal components for core/non-core reporting for Cash Assistance and Food Assistance.

children.title: Scheduling Actions and Services
children.summary: This section explains how outcome workers select and schedule plan activities, including recommended and non-suggested services, actions, and referrals based on self-sufficiency assessments. It details editable scheduling fields such as start/end dates, frequency, hours/days per week, estimated cost, and federal component. It also covers managing external referrals, including provider details and referral letters, and describes how activities are associated with federal components for core/non-core reporting.

children.title: Generating External Referrals
children.summary: Explains how outcome workers generate external referrals in Cúram Income Support for Return to Work. The system lets workers record provider details, write and optionally auto-send or print referral letters to clients and/or providers, and associate referrals with assessment factors. It also distinguishes external referrals from internal outcomes services referrals that are not plan items. Nearby context covers selecting/scheduling work activities and associating plan activities with federal components for reporting.

children.title: Associating Plan Activities with Federal Components
Understanding Cash Assistance Federal Components
children.summary: Explains how participation hours are categorized into Core, Non-Core, or Other Hours based on assigned federal components, then lists the initial Cash Assistance and Food Assistance federal components and their classifications. Cash Assistance core components include employment, training, work experience, job search/readiness, community service, child care for community service, and vocational education; non-core includes job skills training and certain education/secondary school activities. Food Assistance core components include work experience/training, on-the-job training, and workfare; the rest are non-core.

Explains how activities in a plan can be linked to federal components to support hour tracking and regulatory reporting. The system provides initial federal component content for Cash Assistance and Food Assistance, and agencies can configure which components are core or non-core. Cash Assistance activities are mapped to components in initial content. For Food Assistance, only the components are provided initially; no activity mapping is included because no initial activities are supplied.

children.title: Understanding Food Assistance Federal Components
children.summary: Lists Food Assistance federal components and classifies each as Core or Non-Core for participation-hour reporting. Core items include Work experience or Training, On-the-Job-Training, and Workfare. Non-Core items include Education, Self-employment Training, Independent Job Search, Job search Training and Support, Vocational Training, Self-initiated Workfare, and WIS. The section helps determine how activity hours are categorized in the Core, Non-Core, or Other Hours columns.

Covers managing client outcome plan activities in Return to Work Outcome Management. Explains what activities are (actions, services with providers, and external referrals), lists predefined A&T actions/services, and describes selecting recommended or non-suggested activities from Self-Sufficiency assessments. Also covers scheduling details, external referral letters/provider info, and associating activities with federal components for core/non-core participation-hour reporting, including initial Cash Assistance and Food Assistance component mappings.

children.title: Approving Plans
children.summary: Explains how outcome workers approve and manage self-sufficiency plans through periodic reassessment, adjusting activities or creating new ones, and creating new agreements when needed. Covers closing an outcome plan when all goals are resolved or the eligibility case closes, with status changing from Open to Closed and status history retained. Also describes client agreements: created, approved, issued, signed, and monitored, sometimes multiple per plan. Includes monitoring eligibility changes and notifications affecting work or program eligibility, including closing plans when Cash Assistance closes.

children.title: Closing an Outcome Plan
children.summary: Explains when and how an outcome plan is closed: it can be closed once all goals/objectives have an attained/not attained outcome or when the related eligibility case closes, changing status from Open to Closed with status history preserved. Also covers related plan management topics: periodic reassessment and plan adjustment, creating/approving client agreements, and monitoring eligibility changes that may trigger notifications or plan closure.

children.title: Managing Client Agreements
children.summary: Explains how client agreements are managed within Return to Work Outcome Management. Covers approving and issuing plan agreements, periodic reassessment and plan adjustment as clients progress, and the fact that a plan may have multiple agreements with different dates for different individuals. Describes closing an outcome plan when all goals have outcomes or the eligibility case closes, and notes status history is tracked. Also mentions monitoring client plans and eligibility changes, including notifications for work and program eligibility updates.

children.title: Monitoring Client Plans
children.summary: Explains how outcome workers monitor client self-sufficiency plans through periodic reassessments, updating activities or creating new ones as needs change, and may create new agreements per agency procedure. Also covers closing an outcome plan when all goals have specified outcomes or the eligibility case closes, with status history retained. Notes that plan agreements track the planning process and are acknowledged by client and worker. Includes related monitoring of eligibility changes via notifications and participation/work eligibility views.

children.title: Monitoring Client Eligibility Changes
Change in Work Eligibility
children.summary: Explains that when a client’s work eligibility changes, the outcome worker is notified. It also notes that work eligibility can be monitored using the Work Eligibility and View Participation Summary features for Cash Assistance and Food Assistance. This section sits within broader plan management guidance covering reassessments, plan/ agreement updates, and handling eligibility changes; if program eligibility changes for Cash Assistance and the product delivery closes, the worker may close the plan.

children.title: Change in Program Eligibility
children.summary: Explains how outcome workers handle program eligibility changes for Cash Assistance. Workers receive notifications when a client’s Cash Assistance eligibility status changes, and if the Cash Assistance product delivery closes, they can close the outcome plan. This section sits within client plan monitoring and eligibility change tracking, alongside reassessments, plan adjustments, and agreement management.

Explains how outcome workers monitor client eligibility changes during plan management. Workers receive notifications when work eligibility or Cash Assistance program eligibility changes, and can also track work eligibility via Work Eligibility and View Participation Summary for Cash Assistance and Food Assistance. If Cash Assistance product delivery closes, the worker may close the outcome plan. This section is part of broader guidance on monitoring plans, reassessing client needs, adjusting activities, and managing agreements as circumstances change.

Explains how outcome workers build, manage, and monitor Return to Work outcome plans. Plans are created from pending referrals in an integrated case, may cover one or multiple referred members, and are based on Self-Sufficiency assessments with client participation and sign-off. Covers assigning activities (actions, services, external referrals), scheduling details, and linking them to core/non-core federal components for reporting. Also describes periodic reassessment, agreements, eligibility change notifications, and closing plans when goals are completed or the eligibility case closes.

children.title: 1.7 Monitoring Client Participation
Introduction
children.summary: Explains monitoring client participation in Return to Work Outcome Management. Covers tracking progress toward goals, maintaining activities, assessing effectiveness and service quality, and ensuring providers are paid. Describes attendance tracking via plan-based rosters, provider self-service or paper submissions, and automatic creation of attendance records. Notes outcome workers can submit rosters too. Also lists configured absence types and distinguishes excused absences (e.g., Authorized Holiday, Medical, Hospitalization) from not excused types such as Did not attend - Not Excused and Unauthorized Holiday.

children.title: Tracking Attendance
Excused Absences and Holidays
children.summary: Explains how client attendance is monitored within Return to Work Outcome Management, including tracking participation, attendance rosters, discrepancies, and service effectiveness. Notes that providers can submit attendance via self-service, paper rosters, or outcome workers, and that Provider Management generates rosters and attendance records. The section specifically lists absence types configured in Provider Management, distinguishing excused absences (e.g., authorized holiday, hospitalization, medical, sickness, bereavement, custody, emergency closing) from not excused types (did not attend—Not Excused, Unauthorized Holiday).

Explains how client attendance is tracked within an outcome plan in Return to Work Outcome Management. Attendance can be submitted via provider self-service, paper rosters, or by an outcome worker, while Provider Management generates rosters and attendance records automatically. The section also covers configuring absence types and identifies excused absences (e.g., authorized holiday, hospitalization, medical, sickness, bereavement, custody, emergency closing) versus not excused absences (Did not attend - Not Excused, Unauthorized Holiday).

children.title: Viewing Participation Summaries
children.summary: Explains how outcome workers view participation summaries for clients on a plan, including monthly and weekly hour breakdowns by program, core/non-core/other hours, attendance status, and whether scheduled hours were met. Notes configurable month reporting rules (week start day and calendar vs full-week month methods). Also introduces discrepancy management: batch checks compare scheduled vs actual attendance on active plans, store issues by activity, and notify workers of potential shortfalls for review.

children.title: Managing Discrepancies
Generating Discrepancies
children.summary: Describes the Discrepancy Batch Process, a scheduled monthly batch in A&T that compares scheduled vs. actual attended hours for Cash Assistance clients on active outcome plans. If participation shortfalls are found, discrepancies are stored at the individual activity level and outcome workers are notified to review affected cases. Captured details may include client name, discrepancy date, activity, period, and scheduled hours.

children.title: Investigating and Resolving Discrepancies
children.summary: This section explains how discrepancy checks are configured and resolved for work activity participation. It covers batch run timing, resolve-by days, Pending Discrepancy Pod limits, and separate paid/unpaid activity checks. It describes how discrepancies are generated when scheduled and actual hours differ, including rules for Cash Assistance non-participation reasons, excused absences, and regulatory limits. It also explains that workers manually review discrepancy records, verify or enter attendance/absence data, update status, and may send advisements to eligibility workers.

children.title: Viewing Discrepancies Over Time
children.summary: Explains how workers can view a client’s past discrepancies for the same activity using A&T, including client/activity details, timing, hours, prior resolution, and comments. Also covers outcome plan monitoring and case reviews: workers regularly review goals, services, and actions, with configurable first-review timing and review frequency, and may use assessment tools and updated Self-Sufficiency Assessments to revise plans. Ends by introducing productivity tools and listing A&T features such as attachments, participants, communications, events, notes, and tasks.

Explains how outcome workers monitor participation and manage discrepancies in A&T. Workers can view monthly or weekly participation summaries, with configurable month/week calculation rules. The section then covers the Discrepancy Batch Process, which runs on a configured date (default the 15th) to compare scheduled vs. actual hours for Cash Assistance clients on active outcome plans. It records activity-level discrepancies, notifies workers, and supports paid/unpaid activity checks, absence and non-participation rules, and configurable resolution settings. Workers manually review, resolve, update status, and may advise eligibility workers.

children.title: Monitoring Client Progress/Case Reviews
children.summary: This section explains how outcome workers monitor client progress through regular case reviews of outcome plans. It covers reviewing goals, objectives, services, and actions, and modifying plan components based on client progress. It notes that each outcome plan can be configured with a first review date and ongoing review frequency. The review process may also use assessment tools, including an updated Self-Sufficiency Assessment, to support decisions about family needs and revised self-sufficiency goals.

Explains how Return to Work Outcome Management monitors client participation: tracking attendance from outcome plans via provider self-service, paper rosters, or worker entry; automatic attendance record creation; and configurable excused vs. unexcused absence types. Shows how workers view monthly/weekly participation summaries, including core/non-core/other hours and reporting rules. Covers discrepancy management with a monthly batch process (default run date 15th) that compares scheduled vs. actual hours for active Cash Assistance plans, records activity-level discrepancies, and supports manual review, resolution, and eligibility-worker advisement.

children.title: 1.8 Using Productivity Tools
Introduction
children.summary: This section explains how outcome workers use A&T to review discrepancies over time and monitor client progress through regular case reviews. It covers what discrepancy history includes, possible resolution types, and how outcome plans can be reviewed, adjusted, and supported by assessment tools such as an updated Self-Sufficiency Assessment. It then introduces the productivity tools chapter, listing key A&T features available from the integrated case: Attachments, Case Participants, Communications, Events, Notes, and Tasks.

children.title: Productivity Features
children.summary: This section introduces productivity tools in Assessment and Tracking (A&T) for outcome workers. It highlights integrated case features that support client outcomes, including attachments, case participants, communications, events, notes, and tasks. It also situates these tools within broader workflows such as reviewing discrepancies over time and monitoring client progress through regular case reviews and updated self-sufficiency assessments.

children.title: Outcome Worker Summaries
children.summary: Describes home-page summaries for outcome workers to quickly find urgent work: assigned referrals, pending referrals searchable by name/DOB, assessments due or overdue, and pending attendance/participation discrepancies. Explains that workers can complete referrals, view office-wide pending referrals, and use configurable assessment deadlines. Also introduces the Outcome Worker Supervisor role, which performs worker functions plus supervisory/escalation responsibilities, with a segue into supervisor summaries.

children.title: Outcome Worker Supervisor
children.summary: Describes the Outcome Worker Supervisor role, which manages the workload of an organizational unit handling outcome worker functions. A supervisor can do all outcome worker tasks, plus supervisory functions and receive task escalations when needed. The section introduces outcome supervisor summaries that help identify work needing attention. It follows the worker summaries content, which includes assigned referrals, finding pending referrals, assessments due or overdue, and pending discrepancies.

children.title: Outcome Supervisor Summaries
children.summary: Describes Outcome Supervisor Summaries for A&T outcome management. It explains that outcome supervisor home pages provide workload-monitoring summaries, separate from other application summaries, to help supervisors identify urgent work. The main summaries are Overdue Assessments, showing self-sufficiency assessments in warning or overdue status with plan and due-date details, and Overdue Discrepancies, showing overdue attendance/participation discrepancies pending worker review. Both are scoped to the logged-in supervisor’s unit and use configurable timeframes.

Section 1.8 introduces A&T productivity tools for outcome workers. It covers reviewing discrepancy history (client/activity details, prior resolution, comments), monitoring client progress through regular outcome plan reviews, and using assessment tools like updated Self-Sufficiency Assessments. It then outlines productivity features in the integrated case: Attachments, Case Participants, Communications, Events, Notes, and Tasks. The section also describes home-page summaries for workers and supervisors, including assigned/pending referrals, assessments due/overdue, pending/overdue discrepancies, and supervisor workload monitoring.

Business-level overview of Cúram Income Support for Return to Work Outcome Management. Introduces the client-centric A&T-based case-management approach for employment/outcomes agencies, intended for business analysts familiar with SEM and Income Support. Covers prerequisites and related guides, then outlines the guide’s scope: referrals, self-sufficiency assessments, advisements, outcome plans, participation tracking, and productivity tools. Also explains key roles (eligibility worker, outcome worker, supervisor), integration with Income Support, sanctions/notifications, attendance monitoring, geocoded work referrals, and support for single or multi-person plans.
